question: Do the top-3 knowledge_recombine.py candidates produce genuinely novel insights when manually analyzed, or are they surface-level restatements?
expect: Knowledge recombination: cross top-3 candidates (L-1130xL-1131, L-1129xL-1131, L-815xL-871). Predict >=1 of 3 recombinations produces an accepted novel insight (new lesson or principle). Pair 1 is highest-scored (114.0) and most likely to succeed. Pair 3 (score=68.0) from older lessons with less shared context is most likely to fail.
actual: 2 of 3 pairs produced accepted novel insights. Pair 1 was already recombined by concurrent session into L-1137 (CONFIRMED). Pair 2 produces a genuinely novel insight about colony anti-attractor mechanisms needing symmetry-typed gates, not uniform action gates. Pair 3 produces a novel insight about naming as massive-mode intervention. Prediction of >=1 CONFIRMED. Prediction that pair 3 would fail was FALSIFIED — it produced the deepest cross-domain bridge.
diff: Expectation was >=1/3 accepted; actual is 2/3 accepted + 1/3 pre-empted (already done). Surprise: pair 3 (lowest score, oldest lessons, cross-domain strategy+unknown) produced a more structurally novel recombination than pair 2. Score ranking does not predict insight depth — it predicts citation overlap, which correlates with but does not determine novelty.

pair_1.shared_citation_context: Both lessons cite L-601 (structural enforcement theorem), L-950 (fixed-point attractor/recursion trap), L-1118 (closed-system convergence), L-1124 (8 protocol symmetries), L-1125 (trail-context-truth cycle), and L-1127 (6 reward channels). The shared context is the full theoretical stack: systems decay without enforcement (L-601), improvement intent defaults to measurement (L-950), closed systems converge (L-1118), symmetries break degeneratively (L-1124), the epistemic cycle is closed (L-1125), and reward channels are Goodharted (L-1127). Both lessons build on this common substrate but from opposite directions: L-1130 discovers the recombination mechanism, L-1131 designs the anti-attractor colony.
pair_1.recombination_insight: PRE-EMPTED: Already recombined into L-1137 by concurrent S463 session. The bridge insight is that recombination candidates ARE the automated M3 (reward targeting) targets that L-1131's colony design needs. The citation graph's missing edges prescribe where colony sessions should route their work. This converts the advisory pattern (run recombine before dispatch) into structural enforcement (declare recombination target at lane opening).
pair_1.novelty_assessment: ACCEPTED (via L-1137). The insight is genuinely novel: neither L-1130 nor L-1131 individually recognizes that the citation graph structure prescribes colony routing. L-1130 sees gaps; L-1131 needs targets; the bridge is that gaps ARE targets. This is a functional composition, not a restatement.

pair_2.shared_citation_context: Both cite L-601 (enforcement), L-950 (recursion trap), L-1118 (closure), L-1124 (symmetry breaks), L-1127 (reward channels). L-1129 adds L-1128 (governance-symmetry mapping) and ISO-5/ISO-27 (positive feedback/broken symmetry). L-1131 adds L-787 (confirmation ratio), PHIL-2/17/22 (identity claims). The shared context is reward-symmetry-enforcement theory; the divergence is that L-1129 classifies interventions by symmetry type while L-1131 prescribes 5 uniform mechanisms.
pair_2.recombination_insight: L-1131's 5 anti-attractor mechanisms (M1 action gate, M2 external injection, M3 reward targeting, M4 symmetry check, M5 TTL) are all treated as uniform creation-time enforcement. But L-1129 proves that interventions must match their symmetry-break type: Goldstone interventions for Goldstone modes, massive-mode interventions for massive modes. Applied to L-1131: M1 (action gate — produce a git diff) is a massive-mode intervention because it fights the measurement-default attractor (L-950), which is a massive mode. M2 (external injection) is also massive-mode because closure (L-1118, 97.4% internal) is a massive-mode break. M3 (reward targeting) is Goldstone because domain allocation is directionally arbitrary. M4 (symmetry check) is Goldstone for domain symmetry but massive for confirmation lock. M5 (TTL) is mode-neutral (it is a termination condition, not an intervention type). The key novel insight: L-1131 treats all 5 mechanisms as equally important, but L-1129's framework reveals they have different expected effectiveness. M1 and M2 fight massive modes and are therefore necessary and sufficient for breaking the attractor. M3 and M4 fight Goldstone modes and are helpful for rebalancing but cannot alone prevent collapse. A colony that implements only M3+M4 will rebalance domains but still default to measurement. A colony that implements only M1+M2 will break the attractor but may not distribute work well. The minimal viable anti-attractor colony requires M1+M2 (massive) + M5 (termination), with M3+M4 as optimization. This is a testable prediction neither lesson makes alone.
pair_2.novelty_assessment: ACCEPTED. This is a genuine recombination: L-1129's symmetry-typed intervention framework applied to L-1131's uniform mechanism list produces a typed, prioritized mechanism hierarchy that neither lesson contains. The prediction (M1+M2 necessary, M3+M4 optional) is falsifiable and non-obvious. L-1131 explicitly says all 5 are needed; L-1129 provides the framework to disagree with that claim.
pair_2.potential_lesson_title: Colony anti-attractor mechanisms are symmetry-typed: M1+M2 fight massive modes (necessary), M3+M4 fight Goldstone modes (optimization)
pair_2.falsification_criterion: Colony with M1+M2+M5 only achieves equal or greater mechanism improvement rate compared to colony with all M1-M5

pair_3.title_a: COMMIT Guarantee Prospective Test — Score Change does not equal Dispatch Change
pair_3.title_b: 5-layer escalation clears all campaign stalls — naming beats ranking
pair_3.shared_citation_context: Both cite L-601 (structural enforcement), L-755 (multi-wave campaigns), L-791 (mode enforcement WARN to ERROR), and L-805 (COMMIT dispatch guarantee). The shared context is the dispatch enforcement escalation stack: voluntary advisory fails (L-601), campaigns need multiple waves (L-755), mode declaration must be enforced (L-791), and score promotion alone cannot redirect behavior (L-805). L-815 is the FALSIFICATION of the score-change hypothesis: promoting from rank 10 to rank 3 does NOT change dispatch behavior. L-871 is the RESOLUTION: naming specific frontiers (layer 5) succeeds where ranking systems (layers 1-4) fail.
pair_3.recombination_insight: L-815 proves score-behavior decoupling: changing a domain's UCB1 rank from #10 to #3 produces zero dispatch change. L-871 proves naming beats ranking: identifying specific frontier IDs bypasses the rank-to-action gap entirely. Neither lesson connects to the L-1124/L-1129 symmetry framework (they predate it by 60+ sessions), but the bridge insight maps naturally: ranking systems are Goldstone-mode interventions (they rotate which domain is 'on top' — the direction is arbitrary, cheap to change), while naming systems are massive-mode interventions (they inject specific energy into a specific location, fighting the attractor that pulls sessions toward high-exploit domains). L-815's falsification result IS the proof that Goldstone interventions cannot fix massive-mode problems, measured 60 sessions before the symmetry framework existed. L-871's naming-beats-ranking finding IS the empirical discovery of massive-mode intervention, stated in dispatch terms rather than physics terms. The novel bridge: the 5-layer escalation stack (L-871) is actually a Goldstone-to-massive-mode escalation — layers 1-4 are increasingly strong Goldstone rotations (score boost, floor, guarantee, reservation), each failing to redirect behavior, until layer 5 (naming = massive-mode injection) breaks through. This means the escalation stack is not 5 equivalent layers of increasing strength; it is 4 layers of the wrong intervention type plus 1 layer of the right type. The implication is architectural: any dispatch system facing score-behavior decoupling should skip layers 1-4 and go directly to naming, because the problem is not insufficient rotation but insufficient mode-matching. The 4 Goldstone layers were necessary to prove the diagnosis, not to solve the problem.
pair_3.novelty_assessment: ACCEPTED. This is the deepest recombination of the three. It retroactively explains a 60-session-old empirical finding (L-815's falsification) using a theoretical framework (L-1124/L-1129 symmetry types) that did not exist when the finding was made. Neither L-815 nor L-871 contains the symmetry interpretation; neither L-1124 nor L-1129 references dispatch escalation. The bridge connects empirical dispatch history to theoretical symmetry physics, producing a falsifiable architectural prescription (skip Goldstone layers, go to naming) that none of the four lessons individually contains. It also reveals that L-871's '5-layer escalation' is actually a 1-layer solution preceded by 4 layers of diagnostic evidence.
pair_3.potential_lesson_title: Dispatch escalation layers 1-4 are Goldstone rotations; only layer 5 (naming) is massive-mode — skip to naming when score-behavior decoupling is detected
pair_3.falsification_criterion: Direct naming (layer 5) without prior Goldstone escalation (layers 1-4) achieves <50% of the dispatch correction that full 5-layer escalation achieves, proving the Goldstone layers are causally necessary not just diagnostic
pair_3.retroactive_bridge_note: This recombination also demonstrates that knowledge_recombine.py can bridge temporal gaps: L-815/L-871 are from S396-S403 (60+ sessions ago) while L-1124/L-1129 are from S460-S461. The tool found a pair that predates the theoretical framework needed to explain it, creating a retroactive explanatory bridge. This is a second-order finding about recombination itself.

conclusion: Knowledge recombination via citation missing-edges produces genuinely novel insights at a rate exceeding the 25% threshold set by F-KNOW1. In this experiment: 3 pairs attempted, 2 accepted as novel (66.7%), 1 pre-empted by concurrent session but also accepted. The tool's score ranking (114.0 > 95.0 > 68.0) correctly predicts shared citation density but does NOT predict insight depth — pair 3 (lowest score) produced the deepest cross-domain bridge by connecting empirical dispatch history (S396-S403) to theoretical symmetry physics (S460-S461). This suggests the scoring function should be amended: temporal distance between lessons may be a positive signal for recombination depth, not noise. Cross-domain pairs with shared enforcement-theory citations (L-601 appeared in all 3 pairs) are particularly fertile because L-601 is a universal substrate that manifests differently in each domain. The recombination process also generates a second-order insight: retroactive explanation (applying newer theory to older data) is a distinct and valuable recombination mode that the current tool does not distinguish from same-era synthesis.

f_know1_cumulative.rate: 100% (n=4: L-1129, L-1137, pair 2 candidate, pair 3 candidate)
f_know1_cumulative.note: Rate inflated by small n and selection of top candidates. True rate will emerge at n>=10 per F-KNOW1 criterion.

scoring_feedback.observation: Score predicts citation overlap, not insight depth. Pair 3 (score=68) produced deeper insight than pair 2 (score=95).
scoring_feedback.hypothesis: Temporal distance between parent lessons may correlate positively with recombination depth. Pairs from different eras bring genuinely independent perspectives.
scoring_feedback.proposed_amendment: Add temporal_distance_bonus = abs(session_a - session_b) / 100 to scoring function. This would promote cross-era recombinations.
scoring_feedback.falsifiable: If temporal-distance-weighted candidates produce lower acceptance rates than proximity-weighted candidates over 20 attempts

second_order_findings: Retroactive explanation (applying newer theory to older data) is a distinct recombination mode worth distinguishing in the tool
L-601 appears as shared citation in all 3 pairs — it is the universal substrate for recombination in this knowledge base
Pre-emption by concurrent sessions is evidence that high-scoring candidates are genuine targets (multiple sessions independently converge on them)
The symmetry-typing framework (L-1124/L-1129) functions as a universal bridge language — it can retroactively explain findings from any domain that involves intervention design
